era_context: The Ming Dynasty is in the later years of the Yongle Emperor's reign (r. 1402–1424). The Hussite Wars are ongoing in Bohemia, with Jan Žižka commanding Hussite forces. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power under Murad II (r. 1421–1451). The Portuguese are beginning systematic exploration of the African coast under Prince Henry the Navigator.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (no contemporary written records)
Northern and Eastern Europe outside major political centers

events.title: Yongle Emperor's Reign: Continued Expansion and Cultural Achievement
events.description: In 1422, the Yongle Emperor was in the later years of his reign, which had been marked by significant military campaigns, the construction of the Forbidden City (completed 1420), and the compilation of the Yongle Encyclopedia. The emperor was also sponsoring Zheng He's treasure voyages, which continued to demonstrate Ming naval power. The Yongle Emperor's reign represented the height of Ming expansionism and cultural achievement, though his successors would eventually curtail these expansionist policies.
events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's reign is well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Jan Žižka's Military Campaigns
events.description: In 1422, Jan Žižka was leading Hussite forces in military campaigns against Catholic armies and rival factions. The Hussite Wars, sparked by the execution of Jan Hus in 1415, were in their early and most radical phase. Žižka's innovative military tactics, particularly the use of wagon fortifications (Wagenburg), were proving effective against larger Catholic armies. The year 1422 saw continued Hussite military success and expansion of their territorial control.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars

events.certainty_note: Contemporary chronicles document Hussite military campaigns in detail.

events.title: Ottoman Consolidation Under Murad II
events.description: Murad II, who became Ottoman Sultan in 1421, was consolidating Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans in 1422. His reign saw the suppression of internal rebellions and the expansion of Ottoman control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. Murad II's military and administrative reforms strengthened the Ottoman state, making it a major regional power. The gradual encirclement of Constantinople was underway.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation under Murad II is well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Zheng He's Treasure Voyages Continue
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's treasure voyages continued in 1422 as part of the Yongle Emperor's program of maritime expansion and diplomatic outreach. These massive expeditions, involving hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, reached the coasts of East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian Ocean. The voyages demonstrated Ming naval supremacy and were intended to establish tributary relationships with distant states.

sources.name: Luo Maodeng, The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Yingya Shenglan)
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and coastal sites

events.certainty_note: The exact dates of Zheng He's voyages are debated by scholars. Contemporary documentation is limited; most records are from later compilations.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration of the African Coast
events.description: Portuguese expeditions under Prince Henry the Navigator continued systematic exploration of the West African coast in 1422. These voyages were motivated by trade, gold, and religious conversion. By 1422, Portuguese knowledge of African geography was gradually expanding, though major breakthroughs like the crossing of Cape Bojador would come later (1434).

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: The exact timeline of early Portuguese exploration is uncertain. Most detailed accounts come from later chronicles.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The Yongle Emperor's reign has been reinterpreted by recent scholarship, which emphasizes the costs of his military campaigns and the eventual curtailment of his expansionist policies by his successors. The significance of Zheng He's voyages has been debated: while traditionally celebrated as demonstrations of Ming power, recent scholarship emphasizes their diplomatic and tributary functions rather than exploration for its own sake. The Portuguese exploration narrative has been complicated by postcolonial scholarship, which notes that early Portuguese expeditions were often unsuccessful and that the 'Age of Exploration' framing obscures the agency and sophistication of African societies.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1422 is similar to previous years: heavily weighted toward East Asia and Europe, with minimal coverage of Africa, the Americas, and other regions. Chinese sources are abundant but retrospective. European sources are contemporary but elite-focused. The 'significance' of events reflects historiographic bias toward literate, centralized states. The year 1422 is not particularly distinctive in the historical record; it is included primarily because it falls within the reign of the Yongle Emperor and the period of the Hussite Wars, which are considered historically significant.

graph_edges.note: Yongle Emperor's reign ended with his death in 1424
Jan Žižka's campaigns continued until his death in 1424
Ottoman consolidation under Murad II was part of the process leading to the fall of Constantinople
Zheng He's voyages were part of a continuous program of Ming maritime expansion
